questions.question: What are microservices and how do they differ from a monolith?
questions.answer:
Microservices is an architectural style where an application is structured as a collection of small, independently deployable services — each running in its own process, communicating via well-defined APIs (HTTP/REST, gRPC, messaging).

Monolith vs Microservices:

AspectMonolithMicroservices
DeploymentOne unit deployed togetherEach service deployed independently
ScalingScale the whole appScale only hot services
TechnologyOne language/frameworkPolyglot (each service chooses)
Team structureShared codebaseOne team owns one service
FailureOne bug can bring down everythingFailure is isolated to a service
ComplexitySimple to develop/debugDistributed systems complexity

In CS_PM_Service, I worked on a .NET 6 microservices setup with 8 workers and separate services. Each service had its own DB, deployed independently. Complexity was high — network calls, service discovery, distributed tracing — but each team could deploy independently without coordinating releases.

followUps.question: When should you NOT use microservices?
followUps.answer: Avoid microservices when: (1) Team is small — the operational overhead (separate deployments, monitoring, service discovery) requires dedicated DevOps capacity. A team of 3 developers can't afford to manage 10 services. (2) Domain is not well understood — drawing service boundaries requires knowing which parts of the domain change independently. Wrong boundaries create distributed monoliths (services that must always deploy together) — the worst of both worlds. (3) Consistency requirements are high — distributed transactions across services are painful. If your use case needs strong consistency across many entities, a monolith with transactions is simpler. (4) Performance is critical — network calls between services add latency. In-process function calls are orders of magnitude faster.

followUps.question: What is the strangler fig pattern for migrating from monolith?
followUps.answer: The strangler fig pattern migrates a monolith to microservices incrementally rather than a risky big-bang rewrite. You place a façade (API gateway or reverse proxy) in front of the monolith, then extract features one by one as new services. The façade routes requests: new services handle extracted features; the monolith handles the rest. Over time the monolith shrinks and the microservices grow — the monolith is "strangled" until it can be decommissioned. The key benefit: the system remains running throughout. Each extraction is a separate, contained migration. If a new service fails, you can route back to the monolith temporarily. This is how most real-world monolith-to-microservices migrations work in practice.

questions.tip: Start with a monolith. Microservices have enormous operational overhead (service discovery, distributed tracing, network partitions, eventual consistency). Only split when you have clear team/scaling boundaries that justify the complexity. Most startups shouldn't use microservices.

questions.question: What is an API Gateway and what does it do?
questions.answer:
An API Gateway is a single entry point for all client requests to a microservices system. Instead of clients calling individual services directly, they call the gateway which routes to the appropriate service.

What the gateway handles:

  • Routing — /api/orders → Order Service, /api/inventory → Inventory Service
  • Authentication — validate JWT once at the gateway instead of in every service
  • Rate limiting — throttle clients before requests reach services
  • SSL termination — HTTPS at the gateway, internal HTTP between services
  • Load balancing — distribute requests across service instances
  • Request aggregation — combine responses from multiple services into one (BFF pattern)
  • Circuit breaking — stop calling a failing service

Options in .NET ecosystem:

  • YARP (Yet Another Reverse Proxy) — Microsoft's .NET-native reverse proxy, highly configurable
  • Ocelot — popular open-source API gateway for .NET
  • Azure API Management — managed cloud gateway

In CS_PM_Service we used YARP as the API gateway — it handled routing and JWT validation centrally, so individual microservices only needed authorization (not authentication).

followUps.question: What is YARP?
followUps.answer: YARP (Yet Another Reverse Proxy) is a high-performance, .NET-native reverse proxy library from Microsoft. Unlike Nginx or HAProxy, YARP is configured in C# code and runs inside your ASP.NET Core process — you can use the full .NET middleware pipeline, DI, and logging. Routes are configured in appsettings.json or code and support hot-reload (no restart needed). YARP supports: request routing by path/host, load balancing across upstream clusters, session affinity, transforms (modify request/response headers), and health checks. It integrates with ASP.NET Core middleware so you can add rate limiting, authentication, or custom logic for specific routes. Used as the API gateway in CS_PM_Service to route and authenticate before forwarding to upstream services.

followUps.question: What is the BFF (Backend for Frontend) pattern?
followUps.answer: BFF (Backend for Frontend) creates a dedicated API gateway/backend per client type — one for the mobile app, one for the web app, one for third-party partners. Each BFF aggregates and transforms microservice responses optimally for its specific client. The mobile BFF returns compact payloads (small screens, limited data); the web BFF returns richer data (full dashboard views). Without BFF, one generic API has to serve all clients — over-fetching for mobile, under-fetching for rich web UIs. BFF also allows each team to evolve their API contract independently. The trade-off: more services to maintain. In FolioTrack I would use BFF to have a web-optimized API (with all portfolio details) and a mobile-optimized API (stripped down for fast loading).

questions.tip: API Gateway is a single point of failure — deploy it with multiple instances behind a load balancer. YARP handles hot-reload of route configuration without restart, which is crucial for zero-downtime routing changes.

questions.question: What is event-driven architecture and how does it apply to microservices?
questions.answer:
In event-driven architecture, services communicate by producing and consuming events (messages) via a message broker (RabbitMQ, Azure Service Bus, Kafka). Services are decoupled — the producer doesn't know who consumes its events.

Key concepts:

  • Event producer — publishes events when something happens ("OrderPlaced", "PaymentProcessed")
  • Message broker — stores and routes events (Azure Service Bus, RabbitMQ)
  • Event consumer — subscribes to events and processes them asynchronously
// Publisher (Order Service) — Azure Service Bus
public async Task PublishOrderPlacedAsync(Order order)
{
    var message = new ServiceBusMessage(
        JsonSerializer.SerializeToUtf8Bytes(
            new OrderPlacedEvent(order.Id, order.CustomerId, order.Total)));
    await _sender.SendMessageAsync(message);
}

// Consumer (Notification Service) — processes event async
public class OrderPlacedConsumer : BackgroundService
{
    protected override async Task ExecuteAsync(CancellationToken ct)
    {
        var processor = _serviceBusClient.CreateProcessor("order-placed-topic");
        processor.ProcessMessageAsync += async args =>
        {
            var evt = args.Message.Body.ToObjectFromJson<OrderPlacedEvent>();
            await _emailService.SendOrderConfirmationAsync(evt.CustomerId, evt.OrderId);
            await args.CompleteMessageAsync(args.Message);
        };
        await processor.StartProcessingAsync(ct);
    }
}

followUps.question: What is eventual consistency?
followUps.answer: Eventual consistency means that after an update, different parts of the system may temporarily show different data — but given enough time (no new updates), all replicas will converge to the same state. In event-driven microservices, a payment service processes a payment and publishes a PaymentProcessed event. The order service consumes it and updates the order status. Between those two moments, the payment service says "paid" but the order service still shows "pending" — that's eventual consistency. Systems must be designed to handle this: show stale data gracefully, avoid making decisions based on data from other services that may be stale, and use compensating transactions if things go wrong. The trade-off for eventual consistency is high availability and partition tolerance (CAP theorem).

followUps.question: What is the Outbox pattern?
followUps.answer: The Outbox pattern solves the dual-write problem: if you save to DB and then publish an event, a crash between those two steps loses the event. The fix: write the event to an Outbox table in the same database transaction as your business data. A separate background job (or the Transactional Outbox relay) polls the Outbox table and publishes events that haven't been sent yet. Once published, the row is marked as processed. This guarantees at-least-once delivery — the event is always published eventually, even if the process crashes. Combined with idempotent message handlers (safe to process twice), this achieves reliable messaging without distributed transactions. Libraries like MassTransit and Wolverine have built-in Outbox support for .NET.

questions.tip: Outbox pattern — solves the dual-write problem: saving to DB and publishing an event are not atomic. If you save and crash before publishing, the event is lost. Solution: write events to an Outbox DB table in the same transaction, then a background job publishes them reliably.

questions.question: What is the Circuit Breaker pattern and why is it important in microservices?
questions.answer:
The Circuit Breaker pattern prevents cascading failures in distributed systems. When a downstream service starts failing, the circuit breaker opens (stops sending requests) and returns a fallback response immediately — without waiting for timeouts that would cascade through the system.

Three states:

  • Closed — normal operation, requests flow through
  • Open — service is failing, requests short-circuit immediately (no calls made)
  • Half-Open — trial state: allow a few requests through to test if service recovered

Implementation with Polly:

// Program.cs
builder.Services.AddHttpClient<IInventoryService, InventoryService>()
    .AddStandardResilienceHandler(options =>
    {
        // Retry: 3 attempts with exponential backoff
        options.Retry.MaxRetryAttempts = 3;
        options.Retry.Delay = TimeSpan.FromMilliseconds(200);
        options.Retry.BackoffType = DelayBackoffType.Exponential;

        // Circuit Breaker
        options.CircuitBreaker.FailureRatio = 0.5;    // open if 50% fail
        options.CircuitBreaker.SamplingDuration = TimeSpan.FromSeconds(10);
        options.CircuitBreaker.BreakDuration = TimeSpan.FromSeconds(30);
    });

In CS_PM_Service, when the payment gateway was down, the circuit breaker opened immediately — preventing the entire order service from hanging with 30-second timeouts on every request.

followUps.question: What is Polly?
followUps.answer: Polly is the standard resilience library for .NET. It provides retry, circuit breaker, timeout, fallback, and bulkhead policies composable via a fluent API. In .NET 8, Polly v8 is integrated with Microsoft.Extensions.Http.Resilience — use AddStandardResilienceHandler() which configures retry + circuit breaker + timeout with sensible production defaults in one call. For custom policies, wrap in a ResiliencePipeline: new ResiliencePipelineBuilder().AddRetry(...).AddCircuitBreaker(...).Build(). Polly is non-negotiable for any service calling external HTTP APIs — without retry and circuit breaker, a transient failure in one service propagates as cascading failures through the entire system.

followUps.question: What is bulkhead isolation?
followUps.answer: Bulkhead isolation limits the resources (threads, connections) one service can consume so failures in one part don't starve the entire system. Named after ship bulkheads — watertight compartments that prevent one hull breach from sinking the ship. In Polly: AddConcurrencyLimiter(maxConcurrentCalls: 10, queueSize: 5) — at most 10 concurrent calls to the downstream service; 5 can queue; additional requests are rejected immediately with a fallback. Without bulkhead: a slow downstream service causes all threads in the calling service to block waiting, exhausting the thread pool and making the calling service unresponsive. With bulkhead: only the isolated thread pool for that dependency is affected; the rest of the application continues normally.

questions.tip: Polly is the standard resilience library for .NET. In .NET 8, use AddStandardResilienceHandler() (from Microsoft.Extensions.Http.Resilience) which configures retry + circuit breaker + timeout with sensible defaults. Don't roll your own retry logic.

questions.question: What is Clean Architecture and how does it relate to microservices?
questions.answer:
Clean Architecture (Robert C. Martin) organizes code into concentric layers where inner layers don't know about outer layers:

  • Domain (innermost) — entities, value objects, domain events. Pure C# — no external dependencies.
  • Application — use cases (commands/queries), interfaces (IRepository), DTOs. Depends only on Domain.
  • Infrastructure — EF Core, HTTP clients, email senders. Implements Application interfaces.
  • Presentation (outermost) — controllers, Razor views. Depends on Application.
MyApp.Domain/          # Entities, Value Objects, Domain Events
  └── Entities/
  └── ValueObjects/
MyApp.Application/     # Use Cases, Interfaces, DTOs
  └── Portfolios/
      └── Commands/
      └── Queries/
  └── Interfaces/
MyApp.Infrastructure/  # EF Core, SMTP, HTTP, etc.
  └── Persistence/
  └── Email/
MyApp.Api/             # Controllers, Middleware, DI wiring

Dependency Rule: Source code dependencies always point inward. Domain knows nothing about EF Core or HTTP. This makes the domain testable in isolation and infrastructure replaceable.

Clean Architecture works at any scale — each microservice can be a Clean Architecture app internally.

followUps.question: What is the Dependency Inversion Principle in Clean Architecture?
followUps.answer: In Clean Architecture, the Dependency Inversion Principle is implemented structurally: the inner Application layer defines interfaces (IPortfolioRepository, IEmailSender), and the outer Infrastructure layer provides the implementations (EfPortfolioRepository, SmtpEmailSender). The Application layer depends on the abstraction (interface) it defines, not on the concrete infrastructure. The Infrastructure layer depends on (implements) the Application's interfaces — so the dependency arrow points inward (infrastructure depends on application, not the reverse). This keeps the domain and application layers free of framework dependencies, making them testable in isolation with fake implementations.

followUps.question: Clean Architecture vs Vertical Slice Architecture?
followUps.answer: Clean Architecture organizes by technical layer — Domain, Application, Infrastructure, Presentation as separate projects. Every feature touches all layers. Works well for domain-rich applications with complex business rules shared across features. Can become hard to navigate as the app grows — adding one feature requires touching 4 projects. Vertical Slice Architecture organizes by feature — each feature (Create Portfolio, List Holdings) has its own folder containing its command, handler, validator, endpoint, and DTOs. No shared layers. Easy to find all code for a feature. Better for teams because features are independent — one developer owns one slice. Trade-off: shared infrastructure code (DbContext, email) still needs a common project. Many modern .NET apps use Vertical Slices with MediatR; large domain-centric apps benefit from Clean Architecture's strict layering.

questions.tip: Vertical Slice Architecture is an alternative — organize by feature (Portfolio creation, Portfolio listing) rather than technical layers. Each feature has its own command, handler, validator, and query in one folder. Scales better with teams than the horizontal Clean Architecture layers.
